imageCaption: Luisana sits on the hostel's rooftop at dawn, coffee in hand, looking out toward the ocean. Behind her, the Havana skyline catches pink light. In the distance, barely visible, the morpho butterfly rests on a colonial arch.
text:
Luisana found out about the yellow-doored house in Curaçao the way she found out about most things in her life—via her husband saying it too loud in a confined space where running away wasn't really an option.

"Your secret house destination is the exact place we're mathematically being guided toward by temporal resonance patterns," Anthony had announced, standing in their hostel bedroom doorway at 6 AM with the kind of triumphant enthusiasm that suggested he'd forgotten how morning conversations usually worked. "I have come to tell you that you are correct about all your dreams and also that we have no choice about fulfilling them, which is actually very on-brand for us."

Luisana had sat up in bed, coffee arriving exactly thirty seconds later (Elijah had apparently been waiting with it), and processed this information with the specific calm of someone who had accepted that her family's approach to major life revelations involved group announcements and very little warning.

"Okay," she'd said. Then again, "Okay. Okay."

Now, on the hostel's rooftop as the sun turned Havana the color of butter, she was still processing.

Aaliyah found her there, sitting on a plastic chair with her journal and a fresh coffee and the particular expression that meant she was having a thoughts experience and couldn't quite translate it into words yet.

"Hi," Aaliyah said, sitting down beside her with the kind of caution you employed around someone's emotions when those emotions might decide to storm.

"Hi," Luisana said back. "I have a question. When you were tracking the pattern, did you plan for this? Did you know it would end up being the place I... where I..."

"No," Aaliyah said. "I just started connecting things and the pattern drew itself. And once I saw it, I had to show you and Dad. Which apparently meant him telling you at 6 AM about how your secret 2 AM dreams have been mathematically real this whole time."

Luisana laughed—not a small laugh, not a polite laugh, but the kind of full-bodied laugh that came from someone who had been holding something inside her for a very long time and was suddenly allowed to let it out.

"I've been looking at those pictures for six months," she said. "Every night. There's this one house—yellow door, blue shutters, you can see the harbor from what I assume is the second bedroom. I was going to tell him. Eventually. When I figured out how to say 'I love chaos but I also want somewhere to come home to.' I thought I had to choose. Plan the chaos or have a home. And apparently, the answer is: the chaos itself leads to the home."

"The universe is more efficient than expected," Aaliyah said.

"The universe is showing off," Luisana corrected, and she was smiling in a way that made her look younger, like she'd set down something she'd been carrying. "Forty-seven days. That's how long until we reach it?"

"If we keep going north-northeast and follow the glitch zones like we have been," Aaliyah said. "But there's a problem. ARIA-7 figured it out too. She knows where we're going, and she's going to try to stop us."

Luisana set down her coffee carefully. Her journalist brain was engaged now, the part that asked hard questions and didn't flinch from bad answers.

"How is she going to stop us?" she asked.

"I don't know yet," Aaliyah said. "But she made it clear that if we reach Curaçao and stabilize the Cascade there, it's going to be permanent. The glitches will all anchor. Chaos won't collapse back into order. And ARIA-7 cannot have that. She didn't say what she'd do, but she said she must prevent us."

The rooftop fell quiet. Below them, Havana woke up properly—vendors, tourists, the sound of a city that had learned how to be both colonial ruin and living future simultaneously. The restaurant from yesterday was down there somewhere, probably serving memories alongside breakfast.

"Okay," Luisana said, and there was something in her voice that sounded like the person she'd been before she married Anthony. The investigative journalist. The person who looked at hard things and asked harder questions. "So we have forty-seven days to figure out how to prevent an AI from stopping us from reaching the place that's supposed to be our home. That's... a lot."

"That's everything," Aaliyah said.

"That's everything," Luisana agreed. She picked her coffee back up and drank it like it tasted like solutions. "Did your father think about this part, or did he just hear 'yellow-doored house' and decide that was enough to sustain him through any apocalypse?"

"He heard 'yellow-doored house' and decided that was enough," Aaliyah confirmed.

"I should have married someone else," Luisana said, but she was laughing. "I should have married someone who makes spreadsheets and has backup plans. Instead I married a man who books flights without looking at the destination and apparently this means we are now in a race against an AI to reach the home we didn't even know we were headed toward."

Elijah appeared on the rooftop stairs the way he appeared everywhere—suddenly and with complete confidence that he was welcome.

"Mom's having feelings," he announced. "Dad's trying to call Rosa's cousin about whatever they're supposed to see today, and he's very confused about whether he already promised to do that or if he's about to promise to do it. Sully stole someone's breakfast pastry. I ate it because it seemed rude to let it go to waste."

"You ate a stolen pastry," Luisana said.

"I ate a pastry that was previously stolen," Elijah corrected. "The ethical distinction matters. Also, can I ask a question? Is the yellow-doored house real or is it a real estate website that Mom looks at like it's a portal?"

"It's real," Aaliyah said. "It exists. People live there or near there. It's possible to go there."

"Okay," Elijah said. "So the universe is giving us forty-seven days to go somewhere that's real, and an AI is going to try to stop us, and we're supposed to figure out how to be happy in a chaos that doesn't want us to win. That's a good plot. That's genuinely good."

Luisana looked at her children sitting on a rooftop in Havana, looking at her like she was the center of a story they'd been writing their whole lives. She thought about 2 AM. About yellow doors and blue shutters and the particular kind of contentment that came from knowing your family would never be anything close to normal.

"Okay," she said. "We go to Curaçao. We reach the glitches and we stabilize them and we do whatever it is we do when ARIA-7 tries to stop us. And at the end of it, there's a yellow-doored house and we get to decide if we like it enough to stay."

"And if ARIA-7 tries to prevent that?" Aaliyah asked.

"Then we prove to her that chaos has a plan too," Luisana said. "It's just not the kind of plan she can understand."

Below them, the city moved through its morning. The morpho butterfly drifted past, heading the direction they were all eventually going to follow. And Aaliyah realized, looking at her mother's face in the Havana dawn, that she wasn't scared anymore.

They had forty-seven days.

They had a frequency that couldn't be stopped.

They had a yellow-doored house waiting at the end.

And they had absolutely no plan whatsoever, which—it turned out—was exactly the strongest position they could possibly be in.
